questions.question: Explain the difference between synchronous and asynchronous replication. Which one provides stronger consistency and why?
keyPoints: Synchronous waits for replica acknowledgment before commit
Asynchronous commits locally and propagates later
Synchronous provides stronger consistency as all copies have data
Trade-off is higher latency in synchronous

questions.question: Define master-slave replication and list two advantages and two limitations of this configuration.
keyPoints: Master handles writes, slaves replicate for reads
Advantages: load balancing for reads, fault tolerance
Limitations: single write point, write bottleneck
Slave cannot handle writes, master failure requires failover

questions.question: What is replication lag? How does it affect data consistency in asynchronous replication systems?
keyPoints: Replication lag is time delay between master commit and replica apply
In asynchronous systems, replicas are temporarily inconsistent
Reads from replicas may return stale data
Critical for applications requiring real-time consistency

questions.question: Explain the concept of automatic failover in database replication. What are RPO and RTO in this context?
keyPoints: Automatic failover promotes replica to master on primary failure
RPO (Recovery Point Objective) = acceptable data loss based on lag
RTO (Recovery Time Objective) = time to restore service
Determines replication strategy choice

questions.question: Differentiate between physical and logical replication. Give one use case where each would be preferred.
keyPoints: Physical: byte-level copy, exact replica, less flexible
Logical: object-level (SQL), more selective replication
Physical preferred: exact database version copy, simpler setup
Logical preferred: cross-version replication, selective tables

questions.question: What is a replication conflict in master-master configuration? Mention two methods to resolve such conflicts.
keyPoints: Conflict occurs when same data written on different masters concurrently
Resolution methods: Last-Write-Wins (timestamp based)
Conflict detection and manual resolution
Application-defined merge rules
Automatic conflict resolution using MVCC

questions.question: Explain the concept of high availability in database replication. Discuss different replication topologies (star, chain, ring) and their advantages and disadvantages.
keyPoints: HA ensures continuous service despite node failures
Star: central master to all replicas - simple but single point of failure
Chain: linked series - less network hops but higher latency
Ring: circular - resilient but complex
Choose based on network structure, latency requirements

questions.question: Explain CAP theorem and its implications for database replication design. How do synchronous and asynchronous replication relate to CAP properties?
keyPoints: CAP: Consistency, Availability, Partition Tolerance - only two guaranteed
Synchronous replication favors consistency + availability (without partitions)
Asynchronous replication favors availability + partition tolerance
Replication design must choose trade-offs based on requirements
Practical systems prioritize availability with eventual consistency

questions.question: Design a database replication strategy for a banking application requiring zero data loss and high availability. Justify your choices for replication mode, topology, and failover mechanism.
keyPoints: Use synchronous replication for zero data loss
Master-slave with hot standby for HA
Automatic failover with minimal RTO
Multiple geographically distributed replicas
Regular failover testing and monitoring
RPO should be zero, RTO minimal

questions.question: Compare master-slave and master-master replication. For each of the following scenarios, recommend appropriate replication type: (a) E-commerce product catalog (b) Distributed order processing system (c) Analytics reporting database
keyPoints: Master-slave: simple, single write point, easier conflict resolution
Master-master: multiple write points, complex conflicts
E-commerce catalog: master-slave (read-heavy, infrequent writes)
Order processing: master-master (distributed writes)
Analytics: master-slave (reads only, tolerate lag)
